content.text: Model Risk Management (MRM) is the discipline of identifying, measuring, controlling, and monitoring the risks that arise from using quantitative models to inform decisions. Originally codified for banking by the Federal Reserve's SR 11-7 guidance (2011) and the OCC's OCC 2011-12, MRM has become the gold standard for governing any consequential algorithmic system. Agentic AI systems — autonomous agents that plan multi-step actions, invoke external tools, and produce outputs that drive business processes — are models in the SR 11-7 sense, and every component of the MRM framework applies: model inventory, risk tiering, governance roles, documentation, independent validation, approval workflows, and ongoing monitoring. Building an agent MRM framework means adapting these proven mechanisms to the unique characteristics of LLM-based systems: emergent behavior, tool-use side effects, prompt sensitivity, and continuous model updates.

attrs.alt: Agent MRM Framework: three-tier risk classification, governance roles (Model Owner, Validator, Risk Steward, MRM Committee), and lifecycle stages from Inventory through Monitoring

content.text: What It Is

An Agent MRM Framework is an organisational governance system that subjects every agentic AI component — the orchestrator, the tool-calling layer, the underlying language model, and the evaluation harness — to the same lifecycle controls that financial institutions apply to credit-scoring models under SR 11-7. The framework produces a maintained
model inventory
, classifies each entry by
risk tier
, assigns accountable
governance roles

, and gates production deployment on independent validation and formal approval. Ongoing monitoring obligations keep the framework alive after deployment, triggering re-validation whenever the model or its operating environment changes materially.

SR 11-7 defines a model as any quantitative method that transforms inputs into outputs used for decision-making. Agentic AI systems clearly qualify: they take prompts and tool responses as inputs and produce actions or recommendations as outputs. The SR 11-7 text recognises that model risk arises from model error and from inappropriate or incorrect use — both failure modes are especially acute for agents whose emergent multi-step reasoning is difficult to inspect and whose tool-use side effects can propagate into external systems.
Why It Matters

Without a structured MRM framework, organisations deploy agents in an ad-hoc manner: a team builds a proof-of-concept, it works in demos, and it gets promoted to production with no formal record of what the model does, who is responsible for it, whether anyone independently checked its outputs, or what threshold would trigger a re-review. That is precisely the situation SR 11-7 was designed to prevent in banking — and the same failure pattern is repeating itself in the agentic AI wave.

The consequences of ungoverned agent deployment are not hypothetical. An agent approved for internal workflow automation that is later connected to customer-facing APIs, a model fine-tuned on new data without re-validation, a tool permission expanded without re-assessment — each of these is a change that in a formal MRM framework would trigger a documented review. Without the framework, they happen invisibly, accumulating undisclosed risk until a failure surfaces in production.
Benefits
Provides a complete, searchable inventory of every agent model in production — answers the question "what AI systems are we running?" instantly.
Risk-tiering focuses scarce validation resources where they matter most: Tier 1 agents get deep scrutiny; Tier 3 drafting assistants get a lighter attestation.
Clear role accountability prevents the "everyone assumes someone else is responsible" failure mode common in cross-functional AI teams.
Formal approval workflows create a paper trail that satisfies audit requirements and external regulatory inquiries under the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and emerging bank AI guidance.
Ongoing monitoring obligations surface model drift and environmental changes before they become production incidents.
Change management gates prevent scope creep from silently upgrading a Tier 3 agent to a Tier 1 use case without re-validation.
Trade-offs
Governance overhead is real: maintaining model documentation, scheduling validations, and running approval committees adds lead time to deployment cycles.

SR 11-7 was designed for parametric models (regression, gradient boosting) whose internals are inspectable. Foundation LLMs are opaque; the framework must adapt to probabilistic, non-deterministic systems where traditional sensitivity analysis is less tractable.
Rapid LLM provider updates (a new model version released monthly) can generate continuous re-validation demand if tiering and materiality thresholds are not calibrated carefully.
Finding validators with both AI/ML expertise and independence from model-building teams is difficult; many organisations lack this talent in-house.
How It Works
The lifecycle moves through six repeating stages. First, any new agentic system is
registered

in the model inventory by the Model Owner before any production traffic is served. Registration captures the model ID, purpose, data flows, tool permissions, and the business process it supports. Second, the Risk Steward assigns a
risk tier
based on output materiality, autonomy level, and regulatory exposure. Third, the Model Owner prepares
model documentation
covering purpose, design choices, limitations, and known failure modes. Fourth, an independent
Model Validator
reviews the documentation and conducts validation testing proportional to the tier. Fifth, the validator issues a
validation report
with findings (if any) and a recommended disposition (approved, conditionally approved, rejected). Sixth, the
MRM Committee
formally approves high-tier models before production deployment. After deployment, the Risk Steward monitors key risk indicators and schedules periodic re-validations.
Architecture
Model Inventory Schema (YAML)

# agent-model-inventory.yaml
# One entry per registered agent model component
models:
  - id: AGT-2024-001
    name: "Customer Support Triage Agent"
    version: "2.1.0"
    status: production            # draft | staging | production | retired
    tier: 1                       # 1=High, 2=Moderate, 3=Low
    owner: "alice@company.com"
    risk_steward: "bob@company.com"
    validator: "carol@company.com"  # must be independent of owner
    purpose: |
      Routes inbound support tickets to the correct queue;
      generates draft responses for Tier-1 issues.
    inputs:
      - customer_message
      - crm_customer_record
      - knowledge_base_search_results
    outputs:
      - queue_assignment          # binding decision
      - draft_response_text       # human reviews before sending
    tools_permitted:
      - crm_lookup
      - knowledge_base_search
      - ticket_routing_write      # side-effecting
    llm_provider: "anthropic"
    llm_model: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
    deployment_date: "2024-03-15"
    last_validation_date: "2024-06-01"
    next_review_date: "2024-09-01"  # quarterly for Tier 1
    findings_open: 1
    materiality_score: 85          # 0-100; >70 => Tier 1 review required
    regulatory_tags:
      - CCPA
      - FCRA                       # if credit-adjacent

  - id: AGT-2024-007
    name: "Internal Report Summariser"
    version: "1.0.3"
    status: production
    tier: 3
    owner: "dave@company.com"
    risk_steward: "bob@company.com"
    validator: null               # Tier 3: owner self-attests annually
    purpose: "Summarises internal sales reports; human always reviews output."
    tools_permitted:
      - document_read             # read-only
    llm_provider: "anthropic"
    llm_model: "claude-3-haiku-20240307"
    deployment_date: "2024-05-01"
    last_review_date: "2024-05-01"
    next_review_date: "2025-05-01"  # annual for Tier 3
When to Use It

Apply a full Agent MRM Framework when your organisation deploys agents that meet any of the following conditions: the agent's output directly influences a binding business decision (credit, insurance, hiring, routing); the agent has write-access tool permissions that can modify external systems; the agent interacts with regulated data (PII, PHI, financial records); you operate in a regulated industry where model governance is already required; or your aggregate agent deployment has grown to the point where informal tracking of "what AI systems are we running" is no longer reliable.

For very early-stage prototypes or purely read-only, human-supervised tools, a lightweight attestation checklist (equivalent to Tier 3 controls) is sufficient. The MRM Framework should scale with risk, not be applied uniformly regardless of consequence.
How to Implement
MRM Tiering Script
The following Python function computes an initial tier assignment from a structured risk questionnaire. A human Risk Steward reviews and may override the computed tier.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import IntEnum

class Tier(IntEnum):
    HIGH = 1
    MODERATE = 2
    LOW = 3

@dataclass
class AgentRiskProfile:
    # Impact dimensions
    output_is_binding_decision: bool     # True if output is acted on without human review
    output_affects_regulated_activity: bool  # credit, insurance, employment, healthcare
    output_is_customer_facing: bool
    agent_has_write_tool_permissions: bool  # can modify external state
    agent_operates_autonomously: bool       # multi-step without human checkpoints
    # Exposure dimensions
    monthly_decisions_count: int
    pii_data_in_context: bool
    reversibility_of_actions: str          # 'easy' | 'hard' | 'irreversible'

def compute_tier(profile: AgentRiskProfile) -> tuple[Tier, int, list[str]]:
    """
    Returns (tier, score, reasons).
    Score 0-100; tier thresholds: >=70 => Tier 1, >=40 => Tier 2, else Tier 3.
    """
    score = 0
    reasons: list[str] = []

    if profile.output_is_binding_decision:
        score += 30
        reasons.append("Binding decision output (+30)")
    if profile.output_affects_regulated_activity:
        score += 25
        reasons.append("Regulated activity affected (+25)")
    if profile.output_is_customer_facing:
        score += 15
        reasons.append("Customer-facing output (+15)")
    if profile.agent_has_write_tool_permissions:
        score += 20
        reasons.append("Write-permission tools (+20)")
    if profile.agent_operates_autonomously:
        score += 10
        reasons.append("Fully autonomous operation (+10)")
    if profile.monthly_decisions_count > 10_000:
        score += 10
        reasons.append("High decision volume >10k/month (+10)")
    if profile.pii_data_in_context:
        score += 5
        reasons.append("PII in context (+5)")
    if profile.reversibility_of_actions == 'irreversible':
        score += 15
        reasons.append("Irreversible actions (+15)")
    elif profile.reversibility_of_actions == 'hard':
        score += 5
        reasons.append("Hard-to-reverse actions (+5)")

    score = min(score, 100)

    if score >= 70:
        tier = Tier.HIGH
    elif score >= 40:
        tier = Tier.MODERATE
    else:
        tier = Tier.LOW

    return tier, score, reasons


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    profile = AgentRiskProfile(
        output_is_binding_decision=True,
        output_affects_regulated_activity=True,
        output_is_customer_facing=True,
        agent_has_write_tool_permissions=False,
        agent_operates_autonomously=False,
        monthly_decisions_count=50_000,
        pii_data_in_context=True,
        reversibility_of_actions='easy',
    )
    tier, score, reasons = compute_tier(profile)
    print(f"Computed tier: {tier.name} (score={score})")
    for r in reasons:
        print(f"  {r}")
    # Output:
    # Computed tier: HIGH (score=85)
    #   Binding decision output (+30)
    #   Regulated activity affected (+25)
    #   Customer-facing output (+15)
    #   High decision volume >10k/month (+10)
    #   PII in context (+5)
Examples
Model Documentation Template (JSON)
Real-World Example

A regional bank building an AI-powered loan pre-qualification agent applied an MRM framework before deploying the system. The tiering script assigned Tier 1 (score 95) based on binding credit-adjacent outputs and regulated-activity exposure. The Model Owner produced documentation covering the system prompt, tool permissions (
credit_bureau_lookup
read-only,
application_status_write

gated), and known limitations (no ECOA adverse-action language generation). An independent validator, reporting to the Chief Risk Officer rather than the Chief AI Officer, ran a two-week validation and raised two findings: the agent's confidence calibration under distribution shift had not been tested, and the prompt lacked explicit instructions for handling joint applicants. Both were remediated before the MRM Committee granted conditional approval with a 30-day monitoring window. The formal paper trail satisfied the bank's internal audit team and provided the starting point for the regulatory examination brief.
Common Mistakes
Registering only the LLM and ignoring the orchestrator, tool layer, and prompt as separate model components requiring documentation.
Assigning the same person as both Model Owner and Model Validator — this destroys the independence principle that makes validation meaningful.
Treating the initial validation as permanent — agents' LLM providers release model updates that can shift behaviour materially, triggering re-validation.
Setting materiality thresholds so high that every agent lands in Tier 3 — this defeats the framework and accumulates hidden Tier 1 risk.
Documenting the prompt at a high level ("uses a system prompt") rather than version-controlling the exact prompt text used in production.
Skipping KRI monitoring after approval — model drift and environmental changes are the primary ongoing risk post-deployment.
Best Practices
Version-control all prompt artifacts alongside code; a new prompt version is a model change and resets the validation clock.

Define materiality thresholds for model changes (e.g., LLM provider swap, >20% change in any KRI, addition of a write-permission tool) that automatically trigger re-validation without waiting for scheduled reviews.
Build the model inventory in a structured datastore (not a spreadsheet) so the Risk Steward can query "all Tier 1 agents due for re-validation in Q3" programmatically.
Treat tool permissions as part of the model specification: adding a new tool is a model change, not just a deployment change.
Write the limitations section of model documentation for an audience that is not the model builder — regulators and auditors need to understand failure modes without deep LLM expertise.
Establish a clear escalation path: KRI breach → Risk Steward notified within 24h → remediation plan within 5 business days → MRM Committee if not resolved in 30 days.
Performance & Scalability

The administrative overhead of MRM scales with the number of registered models and their tier distribution. A portfolio of 20 models with a 20/40/40 Tier 1/2/3 split requires approximately one full-time-equivalent model risk analyst to manage documentation, coordinate validations, and run committee reporting. At 100+ models, dedicated model risk tooling (GRC platforms with model inventory modules, or purpose-built AI governance tools) becomes necessary to maintain review schedules and track open findings.

Validation throughput is often the bottleneck. Independent validators are a scarce resource. Organisations mitigate this by implementing a proportionality principle: Tier 3 validations are owner-attested (no validator time required), Tier 2 validations use a standardised checklist-driven approach that a trained analyst completes in 1-2 days, and Tier 1 validations receive full validator engagement (1-2 weeks). This tiered effort prevents the validation function from becoming a bottleneck for low-risk agents.
Tooling & Ecosystem
ServiceNow GRC / Archer: enterprise GRC platforms with model inventory modules that can be extended to track agent components and validation status.
Holistic AI, Credo AI, Fiddler AI: purpose-built AI governance platforms that provide model inventory, risk scoring, and monitoring dashboards out of the box.
MLflow Model Registry: lightweight model versioning that can be adapted to store documentation artifacts and approval state alongside model artifacts.
Git + structured YAML (as shown above): a simple, auditable approach for small portfolios; every inventory change is a commit with an author and timestamp.
Datadog / Grafana: monitoring stacks where KRI dashboards are implemented as standard metrics dashboards with threshold-based alerting.
Comparison with Alternatives
NIST AI RMF (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage):
broader risk management framework; less prescriptive on role accountability and approval workflows than SR 11-7. MRM fills in the procedural specifics.
EU AI Act conformity assessment:
regulator-facing compliance process for high-risk AI; MRM is the internal governance discipline that generates the evidence needed for conformity assessment.
Algorithmic Impact Assessment:
a point-in-time pre-deployment review of societal impact; MRM is an ongoing lifecycle discipline that subsumes and extends AIA.
Software SDLC controls:
cover code quality, testing, and deployment. MRM adds the dimension of decision-quality governance that SDLC frameworks do not address.
Security & Reliability

MRM and security governance are complementary but distinct. MRM focuses on decision quality and process integrity; security focuses on confidentiality, integrity, and availability. An agent MRM framework should cross-reference the security posture of each model: a Tier 1 agent with write-permission tools that has not had a prompt injection assessment is carrying both MRM risk (output quality) and security risk (adversarial manipulation of decisions).

The model documentation template should include a field for security review status, and the MRM Committee approval should gate on a clean security assessment for Tier 1 systems. The reliability dimension of MRM covers availability and degraded-mode behaviour: what does the agent do when the LLM API is unavailable or returns an error? A model with no fallback that silently drops decisions is a reliability failure; the monitoring plan must include availability SLI coverage.
Mental Model
Think of an Agent MRM Framework as the
airworthiness certificate system for AI systems

. An aircraft does not fly because it was built by competent engineers — it flies because it passed independent inspection, was granted a type certificate, is operated within its approved envelope, and is subject to mandatory periodic maintenance checks. The certificate is not a bureaucratic formality; it is the institutional memory of everything that was reviewed and found acceptable. The moment the aircraft is modified in a material way (new engine, structural change), the certificate must be renewed.

Apply the same logic to agents: the validation report is the inspection record, the MRM Committee approval is the type certificate, the model inventory is the fleet registry, and the monitoring KRIs are the continuous airworthiness programme. A prompt change is a maintenance action that may or may not require re-certification depending on materiality — just as replacing a seat cushion and replacing an engine both require maintenance records but only one requires a new airworthiness certificate.
Key Takeaways
SR 11-7's model definition explicitly covers agentic AI; the MRM framework is not optional for regulated industries deploying agents with consequential outputs.
Risk tiering (Tier 1/2/3) is the lever that makes MRM proportional — not every agent needs a full validation, but every agent needs to be in the inventory with an assigned tier.

The three critical governance roles — Model Owner, Model Validator, Risk Steward — must be distinct people with clear accountability; conflation destroys the independence that makes each role meaningful.
Prompt changes, LLM provider updates, and new tool permissions are all model changes that must flow through the change management gate.
Ongoing monitoring (KRI dashboards, escalation triggers, scheduled re-validations) is where MRM creates continuous value — the initial approval is a starting point, not an endpoint.
